text: Complications in robotic-assisted colorectal surgery. The authors have no disclosure. From December, two thousand eleven to June, two thousand nineteen, four hundred robotic-assisted colorectal operations were performed by a single surgeon at Taipei Medical University Hospital. All procedures were using Da Vinci SI or XI operation system. There were one hundred eighty seven women and two hundred thirteen men with an average age of fifty six point eight year old. The mean body mass index was twenty three point eight kilogram per square meter. Rectal surgery accounted for seventy four point eight percent of robotic assisted surgery.

Robotic assisted surgery complications include mechanical or equipment failures, user errors, maintenance issues, and others. Tip cover breakage. During the operation, smoke is emitted from different locations of the motor polar forceps instead of just the tip. The surgeon should notice that there's tip cover breakage Tip-cover breakage may occur due to long-term use or overheating. There is a potential risk that the tissue or small intestine are damaged due to thermal injury, which may lead to serious morbidity and even death.

During a low anterior resection, the surgeon uses a robotic endo-wrist stapler to clamp the tissue. As the firing process begins, an alert indicates a stapler malfunction, with the blade potentially exposed and the transaction incomplete. The surgeon removes the stapler to inspect it, discovering a fallen piece of the instrument within the patient's body, which is then retrieved.

The surgeon uses a single-port approach to perform anterior resection, employing one grip for traction on the mesentery and an endo-wrist stapler to ligate the inferior mesenteric artery. After firing the stapler, it is observed that the ureter has been accidentally transected, highlighting the need for careful separation and protection of the ureter during the procedure.

During the dissection of locally advanced rectal cancer, presacral bleeding occurs. The surgical team attempts to secure the bleeding points using packing and compression methods, but the bleeding persists, leading to a conversion to open surgery.

The surgeon encounters soft tissue entrapment at the joint of a surgical instrument, attempting to release it by adjusting the angle. The table assistant forcefully pulls the tissue, risking trauma and bleeding. Meanwhile, an anal tube is inserted for decompression of the extended rectum. The tube is accidentally clamped and transected along with the rectum when staples are fired.

Fail of device deassembly. In this video, the surgeon was performing a lateral dissection and trying to expose the ureter. It went smoothly at the beginning. Suddenly, all equipments were locked. The reason was that the patient card's power cord was kicked off by accident. When this happens, the surgeon might be caught off guard, but it is crucial to respond quickly and precisely. Otherwise, crisis such as passive bleeding might happen if the instruments are locked during hemostasis or adhesiolysis. Sword fighting. When surgeons operate, they may encounter uncontrolled arms. The surgeon controls the arm while seated at the computer console near the operating table. External collisions occurs when the instruments are inserted through ports that are too close together. While the console gives the surgeon a high-definition magnified, three-D view of the surgical sites, the surgeon won't be able to notice that the arms with surgical instruments are overlapped, which causes sword fighting. In this case, unnecessary tension might be built up. It could potentially increase the risk of arm jumping, causing the instrument to puncture an artery. The report in did a retrospective study of years of FDA data on adverse events in robotic surgery. There are death, which is of the reports. Among patient injuries, device failures are accounted for

Appropriate work processes should be planned to minimize the possibility of complications, including, first, standardized training program and conditioning guidelines, such as teacher-residents, fellows, and faculties. Second, strict internal audits and reporting system, by developing a more uniform system of overall reporting. In addition, proper disclosure of risks to patients is crucial, including possible robotic-related complications or potential conversions to open surgery.
